stem: An OTA is working with a client who has a C6 SCI on self-feeding. Which adaptive equipment is MOST appropriate?
options: Universal cuff with built-up handle utensils
rationale: C6 SCI preserves wrist extensors but lacks finger flexion/grip. A universal cuff straps utensils to the hand, and built-up handles compensate for absent grasp.

stem: Which frame of reference emphasizes the use of purposeful activity to develop adaptive responses for sensory processing?
rationale: Ayres Sensory Integration uses purposeful, child-directed activities that provide enhanced sensory input to develop adaptive responses and improve sensory processing and praxis.

stem: A client with rheumatoid arthritis reports morning stiffness lasting 2 hours. Which intervention is MOST appropriate before ADL training?
options: Paraffin wax bath to hands followed by gentle AROM
rationale: Paraffin wax provides superficial heat reducing stiffness and pain in RA. Following with gentle AROM prepares joints for functional activity. Avoid aggressive stretching during flare.

stem: An OTA is fabricating a resting hand splint for a client with flexor tendon repair. What position should the wrist be in?
options: 20-30° flexion to reduce tension on the repaired tendon
rationale: A resting hand splint post-flexor tendon repair positions the wrist in 20-30° flexion, MCPs in 50-70° flexion, and IPs in extension to reduce tension on the repair.

stem: Which cognitive screening tool assesses orientation, memory, attention, language, and visuospatial skills in a brief format?
rationale: The MoCA is a brief cognitive screening tool (10 minutes) assessing multiple domains: attention, concentration, executive function, memory, language, visuospatial, and orientation. Score <26 suggests impairment.

stem: A client with a CVA demonstrates unilateral neglect on the left side. Which compensatory strategy should the OTA teach?
options: Place all items on the left side
Teach scanning techniques: systematically turn head left, use anchoring cues (bright tape) on the left margin
Only work on the right side
rationale: Left neglect compensation: teach systematic left scanning, use anchoring cues (colored tape/line) on the left margin, position important items initially on the right and gradually shift left.

stem: An OTA is training a client with bilateral hip replacements (posterior approach) in lower body dressing. Which technique is correct?
options: Bend forward to put on pants normally
Use a dressing stick and sock aid; bring clothing to the feet using long-handled aids without bending hips past 90°
Stand on one leg to dress
rationale: Bilateral posterior THR: use long-handled dressing aids (dressing stick, sock aid, long-handled shoe horn) to avoid hip flexion >90°, adduction past midline, and internal rotation.

stem: Which developmental milestone is expected at 6 months of age?
options: Sitting independently with hand support, reaching for and grasping objects
rationale: At 6 months: sits with hand support (propping), reaches and grasps objects (palmar grasp), transfers objects between hands, and begins solid food introduction.

stem: A client with Parkinson's disease has micrographia. Which compensatory strategy is MOST effective?
options: Use lined paper with wider spacing, conscious effort to write large letters, and use a weighted pen for proprioceptive feedback
Switch to writing with the non-dominant hand
rationale: Micrographia compensation: lined paper (visual cues), deliberate large letter formation, weighted pen (proprioceptive input), practice with conscious attention to letter size.

stem: An OTA is evaluating a child's fine motor skills. Which assessment tool is standardized for this purpose?
rationale: The PDMS-2 is a standardized assessment of gross and fine motor skills for children birth-5 years. Fine motor subtests include grasping and visual-motor integration.

stem: Which splint is used to prevent ulnar drift in a client with rheumatoid arthritis?
options: MCP ulnar deviation splint (ulnar drift splint) that aligns the MCPs in neutral
rationale: An ulnar drift splint provides a radial force at the MCPs to counteract the ulnar deviation deformity in RA. It is worn during functional activities to maintain alignment.

stem: A client with a traumatic brain injury (TBI) is in Rancho Level V (confused, inappropriate). What is the OTA's primary focus?
options: Structured environment with simple, repetitive activities; minimize overstimulation; maintain safety
rationale: Rancho Level V: the client is confused and responds to simple commands but is highly distractible. Provide structured, consistent routines, reduce stimulation, and focus on basic ADL participation.

stem: An OTA is teaching energy conservation techniques to a client with COPD. Which strategy is MOST important?
options: Plan and prioritize tasks, use pacing (work-rest cycles), sit during activities when possible, and organize workstations at waist height
Complete all tasks in the morning
rationale: Energy conservation: plan/prioritize, pace with rest breaks, sit when possible, organize frequently used items within reach, use lightweight tools, and exhale during exertion.

stem: Which grasp pattern develops FIRST in infancy?
rationale: Grasp development: reflexive palmar grasp (birth) → voluntary palmar grasp (4-5 months) → radial palmar (6-7 months) → inferior pincer (7-8 months) → superior pincer (9-12 months).

stem: A client with carpal tunnel syndrome reports numbness in the thumb, index, and middle fingers. Which nerve is compressed?
options: Median nerve at the carpal tunnel
rationale: The median nerve is compressed at the carpal tunnel (under the transverse carpal ligament). It innervates the thenar muscles and provides sensation to the palmar thumb, index, middle, and radial half of ring finger.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has ideational apraxia. Which intervention approach is BEST?
options: Break activities into individual steps with visual cues, provide errorless learning, and use hand-over-hand guidance
Have the client figure it out independently
rationale: Ideational apraxia (difficulty with the concept/plan of movement): break tasks into steps, provide visual cue cards for sequencing, use errorless learning, and give physical guidance as needed.

stem: Which type of muscle contraction occurs when the OTA assists a client in lowering a cup slowly to the table?
options: Eccentric contraction of the elbow flexors
rationale: Lowering a cup = eccentric contraction of the biceps/elbow flexors (they lengthen under load to control the lowering against gravity). This requires more control than concentric contraction.

stem: A client with a flexor tendon repair (Zone II) is on a Duran protocol. What does this involve?
options: Controlled passive flexion and active extension within a dorsal blocking splint for the first 4 weeks
rationale: The Duran protocol: dorsal blocking splint, controlled passive flexion of DIP and PIP within the splint, active extension to the dorsal block. This prevents adhesions while protecting the repair.

stem: An OTA is teaching a client with a recent CVA to perform a standing pivot transfer. Where should the OTA position themselves?
options: On the affected (hemiplegic) side, slightly in front, using a gait belt
rationale: Position on the affected side to support and guard the weaker side. Use a gait belt for safety. The client leads with the stronger side and pivots toward the stronger side.

stem: Which standardized assessment measures a client's performance of basic ADLs and IADLs?
rationale: The KELS evaluates living skills across self-care, safety, health, money management, transportation, and work. It is used to determine the client's ability to live independently.

stem: A client with a burn injury on the anterior surface of the elbow should be splinted in which position?
options: Full elbow extension to prevent flexion contracture
rationale: Burns on the flexor surface (anterior elbow) tend to contract into flexion. Splint in the anti-deformity position (full extension) to prevent flexion contracture.

stem: When documenting an OTA treatment session, which element is required by Medicare?
options: Skilled intervention provided, client response, objective measurements, progress toward functional goals, and medical necessity
rationale: Medicare requires documentation of skilled services (justification), objective measurements, client response, progress toward functional goals, and medical necessity for continued treatment.

stem: An OTA notices a pressure ulcer developing on a client's sacrum in a wheelchair. What is the FIRST action?
options: Reposition the client immediately, assess cushion and seating, implement pressure relief schedule, and report to the OT
rationale: Stage I pressure injury (non-blanchable erythema): reposition immediately, assess wheelchair cushion adequacy, establish weight-shift schedule, and report to the OT/nursing for wound assessment.

stem: Which hand position is MOST functional for daily activities and should be maintained through splinting when appropriate?
options: Functional position: wrist 20-30° extension, MCPs 45-70° flexion, IPs slightly flexed, thumb in opposition
rationale: The functional hand position (position of function) allows for grasp and release: wrist extension (20-30°), MCP flexion (45-70°), IP slight flexion, and thumb in palmar abduction/opposition.

stem: A client with early-stage Alzheimer's disease is having difficulty with meal preparation. Which approach is MOST appropriate?
options: Simplify recipes, provide written step-by-step instructions with pictures, set up ingredients in order, and supervise for safety
Remove the client from the kitchen entirely
rationale: Early dementia: simplify tasks, provide external cues (written/visual), set up the environment for success, supervise for safety, and maintain participation to preserve skills and self-esteem.

stem: Which type of contracture is MOST common after a distal radius fracture treated with casting?
options: Wrist and finger flexion/stiffness with loss of forearm supination
rationale: Distal radius fracture (Colles') with casting commonly leads to wrist stiffness, finger stiffness, and forearm rotation limitations due to immobilization. Early AROM of uninvolved joints prevents this.

stem: An OTA is working with a child who has difficulty with handwriting due to poor proprioception. Which activity would address this?
options: Writing on a vertical surface (chalkboard), using resistive media (clay letters, sandpaper tracing), and weighted pencil grips
rationale: Proprioceptive input for handwriting: vertical surfaces (gravity resistance), resistive media (deep pressure input), weighted pencils, and activities that increase body awareness.

stem: A client with lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) should avoid which movement during ADLs?
options: Forceful gripping with wrist extension (e.g., wringing a towel, lifting with palm down)
rationale: Lateral epicondylitis involves the wrist extensor origin at the lateral epicondyle. Avoid forceful gripping with wrist extension. Use ergonomic modifications and counterforce bracing.

stem: Which theory of motor learning suggests that practicing in variable conditions leads to better transfer of skills?
options: Schmidt's schema theory (variable practice promotes generalization)
rationale: Schmidt's schema theory: variable practice conditions develop generalized motor programs (schemas) that transfer better to novel situations than constant practice conditions.

stem: An OTA is fabricating a thumb spica splint for de Quervain's tenosynovitis. Which structures must be immobilized?
options: The wrist and thumb CMC/MCP joints, leaving the IP joint free
rationale: De Quervain's involves the APL and EPB tendons at the first dorsal compartment. A thumb spica splint immobilizes the wrist and thumb CMC/MCP, keeping the IP free for function.

stem: A client is performing an activity that requires sustained attention for 15 minutes. This is an example of which type of attention?
rationale: Sustained attention: maintaining focus on a task over time without drifting. Selective: focusing amid distractions. Alternating: shifting between tasks. Divided: attending to multiple tasks simultaneously.

stem: An OTA working in home health notices signs of elder abuse (unexplained bruises, fearful behavior). What is the REQUIRED action?
options: Report to the appropriate adult protective services agency as a mandated reporter
Ignore it — not the OTA's responsibility
rationale: OTAs are mandated reporters. Suspected elder abuse must be reported to adult protective services (APS) immediately. Reporting is required by law regardless of the source of abuse.

stem: Which intervention is MOST appropriate for a client with hemianopsia who has difficulty reading?
options: Teach anchoring strategy (bright line at left margin), use a typoscope, and practice systematic scanning
rationale: Hemianopsia reading strategies: anchor at the affected-side margin, use a typoscope (reading guide), systematic scanning training, and gradually increase reading complexity.

stem: A client with COPD needs to perform shower activities. Which energy conservation modification is MOST helpful?
options: Use a shower chair, handheld showerhead, organize toiletries within reach, and allow rest breaks
rationale: COPD shower modifications: seated position (shower chair reduces energy expenditure 25%), handheld showerhead, organized supplies, terry cloth robe for drying, and pursed-lip breathing throughout.

stem: Which assessment evaluates a client's visual perception including form constancy, figure-ground, and spatial relations?
rationale: The MVPT evaluates visual perceptual skills without motor demands: spatial relationships, visual discrimination, figure-ground, visual closure, and visual memory.

stem: An OTA is teaching a client with hemiplegia to don a button-up shirt using one-handed technique. What is the correct sequence?
options: Place shirt on lap, insert affected arm first, bring shirt around back, insert unaffected arm, then button from bottom up
rationale: One-handed dressing (button shirt): affected arm into sleeve first, pull over shoulder, reach behind for shirt, insert unaffected arm, then button. Reverse for doffing (unaffected arm out first).

stem: A client with a transtibial amputation needs to learn to don a prosthesis with a suction suspension. What is the key step?
options: Apply prosthetic sock, insert the residual limb, expel air through the one-way valve to create suction seal
rationale: Suction suspension: pull sock through valve port, insert residual limb, push in fully while air expels through the one-way valve creating vacuum seal, then remove pull sock through the valve.

stem: Which joint protection principle is MOST important for a client with osteoarthritis of the hands during daily activities?
options: Use the largest joints possible, distribute force over multiple joints, avoid positions of deformity
rationale: Joint protection: use largest joints available, distribute forces broadly, avoid prolonged static positions, respect pain, use ergonomic tools, maintain ROM, and avoid positions that stress deformed joints.

stem: An OTA is performing a kitchen assessment for a client who uses a wheelchair. What is the minimum countertop clearance height for wheelchair access?
options: 28-30 inches clear knee space under the counter
rationale: ADA kitchen standards: minimum 28-30 inches clearance under counters for wheelchair knee space, counter height 28-34 inches, clear floor space 30×48 inches at appliances.

stem: A client with a peripheral nerve injury at the wrist (ulnar nerve) demonstrates which functional deficit?
options: Clawing of the ring and little fingers, weak grip strength, and loss of lateral pinch
rationale: Ulnar nerve injury at the wrist: clawing of ring/little fingers (hyperextension MCPs, flexion IPs), loss of finger abduction/adduction, weak grip, and impaired lateral (key) pinch.

stem: Which wheelchair propulsion technique reduces the risk of repetitive strain injury to the upper extremities?
options: Long, smooth semicircular strokes with a natural return path below the push rim
Using only the fingertips on the rim
rationale: Efficient propulsion: long, smooth strokes maximize distance per push, semicircular recovery below the rim reduces repetitive stress, and proper seat height optimizes biomechanics.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS) Type I of the hand. Which intervention approach is indicated?
options: Stress loading (scrubbing/carrying), desensitization, contrast baths, gentle AROM, and edema management
rationale: CRPS treatment: stress loading program (scrubbing/carrying activates without passive motion), desensitization, contrast baths, gentle AROM, edema management, and mirror therapy.

stem: A client with dysphagia is on a mechanically altered diet. Which food consistency is typically recommended?
options: Pureed, ground, or soft foods that require minimal chewing; liquids may be thickened
rationale: Mechanically altered diets include pureed, ground, or soft foods requiring minimal chewing, based on the client's swallowing ability. The SLP determines appropriate diet level and liquid consistency.

stem: Which theory emphasizes the importance of occupational engagement for health and well-being?
options: Occupational adaptation theory — health is maintained through engagement in meaningful occupation
rationale: Occupational adaptation theory views occupational engagement as essential for health: the client adapts through meaningful activity, developing mastery and adaptive capacity.

stem: An OTA is working with a client on money management skills after TBI. Which cognitive skills are being targeted?
options: Executive functions: planning, sequencing, calculation, judgment, and problem-solving
rationale: Money management requires multiple cognitive skills: calculation, sequencing, planning, judgment, working memory, and problem-solving — all higher-level executive functions.

stem: A child with autism spectrum disorder has difficulty with transitions between activities. Which strategy is MOST effective?
options: Use visual schedules, countdown timers, social stories, and transition objects to prepare for the change
rationale: ASD transition strategies: visual schedules (predictability), countdown timers (anticipation), social stories (understanding), transition objects (comfort), and first-then boards (motivation).

stem: What is the primary purpose of a dynamic extension splint for the hand?
options: Provide a low-load prolonged stretch to increase passive extension ROM of stiff flexed fingers through rubber band traction
rationale: Dynamic extension splints use outriggers and rubber bands to provide low-load prolonged stretch (LLPS) to flexion contractures of the MCP or IP joints, gradually increasing extension ROM.

stem: An OTA needs to communicate a change in the client's functional status to the OT. Which communication format is MOST effective?
options: SBAR format: Situation (what's happening), Background (context), Assessment (your analysis), Recommendation (what you suggest)
Wait until the next team meeting
rationale: SBAR provides structured, concise communication: what's happening now, relevant background, the OTA's clinical assessment, and recommended action for the OT to consider.

stem: Which splint is used to maintain the thumb web space and prevent thumb adduction contracture?
options: C-bar or thumb web spacer splint
rationale: A C-bar (thumb web spacer) maintains the first web space in abduction, preventing the thumb adduction contracture that limits opposition and functional grasp.

stem: A geriatric client is at risk for falls during bathroom activities. Which environmental modification is MOST important?
options: Grab bars near the toilet and shower, non-slip bath mat, raised toilet seat, and adequate lighting
rationale: Bathroom safety: grab bars (toilet, shower), non-slip surfaces (mat, strips), raised toilet seat, shower chair/bench, handheld showerhead, nightlight, and adequate lighting.

stem: An OTA is training a client with a below-elbow amputation to use a body-powered prosthesis. What is the primary control mechanism?
options: Shoulder flexion and scapular abduction via a cable and harness system
rationale: Body-powered prostheses: a cable and harness system translates shoulder flexion and scapular protraction into terminal device (hook/hand) opening. Release allows elastic closure.

stem: Which cognitive rehabilitation approach uses external aids (calendars, alarms, checklists) to compensate for memory deficits?
options: Compensatory/adaptive approach using external memory aids
rationale: The compensatory approach uses external aids to bypass the impaired cognitive function: calendars, alarms, smartphone reminders, checklists, labeled drawers, and routine structures.

stem: A client with a C7 SCI has preserved triceps function. Which ADL activity can they perform independently?
options: Wheelchair transfers, some self-feeding, dressing upper body, and wheelchair mobility
rationale: C7 SCI (triceps, wrist extensors, finger extensors): independent transfers (depression), self-feeding with minimal equipment, upper body dressing, wheelchair mobility, and some lower body dressing.

stem: An OTA is educating caregivers of a client with dementia about effective communication. Which strategy is MOST important?
options: Use simple, short sentences; approach from the front; make eye contact; use calm, reassuring tone; avoid correcting or arguing
rationale: Dementia communication: face the person, use short sentences, speak slowly and clearly, avoid correcting, reduce background noise, use visual cues, and maintain a calm, reassuring approach.

stem: Which assessment measures functional independence in self-care, mobility, and communication for rehabilitation clients?
options: FIM (Functional Independence Measure) — 18-item assessment rated on 7-point scale
rationale: The FIM measures 18 items across motor (13) and cognitive (5) domains on a 1-7 scale (total dependence to complete independence). Total score ranges from 18-126.

stem: A client with a rotator cuff tear has difficulty reaching overhead. Which compensatory strategy should the OTA teach?
options: Use reachers for high items, rearrange frequently used items to waist-chest height, and use long-handled tools
Only lift with the injured arm
rationale: Compensatory strategies for overhead limitations: reachers, reorganize storage (frequently used items at waist-chest height), long-handled tools, and bilateral activities when possible.

stem: Which type of play is characteristic of a 2-year-old child?
options: Parallel play — playing alongside but not with other children
rationale: Play development: solitary (0-2 years) → parallel play (2-3 years, playing alongside others without interaction) → associative (3-4 years) → cooperative (4+ years).

stem: An OTA is treating a client with trigger finger (stenosing tenosynovitis). Which conservative intervention is appropriate?
options: Buddy taping, gentle tendon gliding exercises, modalities for inflammation, and avoiding repetitive gripping
rationale: Trigger finger conservative treatment: rest from aggravating activities, gentle tendon gliding exercises, anti-inflammatory modalities, buddy taping for support, and ergonomic modifications.

stem: A client requires hip precautions after an anterior approach total hip replacement. Which movements are RESTRICTED?
options: Hip extension, external rotation, and adduction
No restrictions — anterior approach has no precautions
rationale: Anterior approach THR precautions: avoid hip hyperextension, external rotation combined with extension, and adduction. These differ from posterior approach precautions.

stem: Which assessment tool evaluates a client's ability to perform IADL tasks in a simulated environment?
options: Executive Function Performance Test (EFPT) — evaluates cooking, phone use, medication management, and bill payment
rationale: The EFPT evaluates executive function through four IADL tasks: simple cooking, telephone use, medication management, and bill payment. It quantifies the amount of cueing needed.

stem: An OTA is providing education on proper body mechanics for a client who performs manual labor. What is the KEY principle?
options: Bend at the waist, reach far
Keep the load close to the body, bend at the knees, maintain neutral spine, avoid twisting while lifting
rationale: Body mechanics: close to body, bend knees (not waist), neutral spine, engage core, avoid twisting, push/pull rather than lift, and take rest breaks during repetitive tasks.

stem: A client with multiple sclerosis reports worsening symptoms with heat exposure. What is this phenomenon called?
options: Uhthoff's phenomenon — transient worsening of neurological symptoms with heat
rationale: Uhthoff's phenomenon: heat exposure temporarily worsens MS symptoms due to decreased nerve conduction velocity in demyelinated nerves. Cooling strategies and exercise in cool environments are recommended.

stem: Which splinting approach is used for a client with a mallet finger injury?
options: Stack splint or DIP extension splint worn continuously for 6-8 weeks to allow healing of the terminal extensor tendon
rationale: Mallet finger (disruption of the terminal extensor tendon): DIP extension splint (stack splint) worn continuously 6-8 weeks. The DIP must not flex during this period to allow healing.

stem: An OTA observes a coworker using a client's phone for personal use. What should the OTA do?
options: Address the boundary violation by reporting it to the supervisor as a breach of professional ethics
Wait to see if it happens again
rationale: Using a client's personal property is a professional boundary violation and potential ethical breach. The OTA has a responsibility to report concerns to maintain professional standards.

stem: A client with a T10 SCI is working on independent dressing. Which technique is appropriate for lower body dressing?
options: Supine with legs positioned, rolling side-to-side to pull pants over hips; use adaptive loops and dressing aids
Having a caregiver dress them always
rationale: T10 SCI lower body dressing: supine in bed, dress legs first, roll side-to-side to position pants over hips. Use adaptive loops on waistbands, buttonhook, and long-handled shoe horn.

stem: Which assessment is used to evaluate handwriting legibility and speed in school-age children?
options: Evaluation Tool of Children's Handwriting (ETCH) or Minnesota Handwriting Assessment
rationale: The ETCH and Minnesota Handwriting Assessment evaluate letter formation, alignment, spacing, size, and writing speed. They identify specific handwriting deficits for targeted intervention.

stem: A client with diabetes has peripheral neuropathy in the feet. What self-care education should the OTA provide?
options: Daily foot inspection, proper footwear, avoid extreme temperatures, moisturize (not between toes), and report changes immediately
rationale: Diabetic neuropathy foot care: daily visual inspection (mirror for soles), well-fitting shoes (no barefoot walking), avoid hot water, moisturize skin, trim nails carefully, and report any wounds/changes.

stem: An OTA is using backward chaining to teach a client with cognitive impairment to put on a shirt. How does this work?
options: Teach the first step and client does the rest
The OTA performs all steps EXCEPT the last one; the client completes the final step. Then the client does the last two steps, etc.
Start from the most difficult step
rationale: Backward chaining: the OTA completes all steps except the last one, which the client performs independently. As competence builds, the client performs the last two steps, then three, etc.

stem: Which sensory modulation strategy is calming for a child with sensory processing disorder who is overstimulated?
options: Deep pressure input (weighted blanket, compression clothing), slow rhythmic rocking, and dim lighting
rationale: Calming sensory strategies: deep pressure (weighted blanket, bear hugs), slow rhythmic vestibular input (rocking), reduced lighting, quiet environment, and proprioceptive activities.

stem: A client post-stroke has shoulder subluxation on the hemiplegic side. Which intervention is MOST appropriate?
options: Support the arm with a sling during upright activities, NMES to the supraspinatus and posterior deltoid, and proper positioning
Pull on the arm to reduce subluxation
rationale: Hemiplegic subluxation: arm support (lap tray, arm trough, or sling during ambulation), NMES to supraspinatus/deltoid, proper positioning (support arm on pillows), and avoid traction on arm.

stem: What is the primary role of the OTA in the discharge planning process?
options: Provide input on functional status, recommend equipment/modifications, educate client/family on HEP, and collaborate with the OT
rationale: The OTA contributes to discharge planning by reporting functional status, recommending adaptive equipment, educating client/family, and collaborating with the OT who makes the final discharge recommendation.

stem: A client with a crush injury to the hand has significant edema. Which intervention reduces edema MOST effectively?
options: Elevation above heart level, retrograde massage, compression wrapping, and active finger pumping exercises
rationale: Edema management: elevation (above heart), retrograde massage (distal to proximal), compression wrapping/glove, active finger pumping (muscle pump), and string wrapping (Coban).

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has ideomotor apraxia. Which cue is MOST effective to elicit the correct motor response?
options: Tactile/proprioceptive cues and hand-over-hand guidance (actual object use in context is better than pantomime)
rationale: Ideomotor apraxia: difficulty executing motor plans despite understanding. Tactile/proprioceptive cues and actual object use in natural context are more effective than verbal or pantomime cues.

stem: A client with low vision needs to organize medications. Which assistive technology is MOST helpful?
options: Talking pill organizer, high-contrast labels with large print, and tactile markings on medication containers
rationale: Low vision medication management: talking pill organizers, high-contrast labels, large print, tactile markings (raised dots, rubber bands), talking watches/timers for schedules.

stem: Which infection control measure is required when treating a client with VRE (vancomycin-resistant enterococcus)?
options: Contact precautions: gown and gloves on entry, dedicated equipment, and hand hygiene with soap and water on exit
rationale: VRE requires contact precautions: gown and gloves upon room entry, hand hygiene (soap and water preferred) on exit, and dedicated/disinfected equipment to prevent spread.

stem: An OTA is teaching wheelchair pressure relief techniques. How often should a client perform weight shifts?
options: Every 15-30 minutes for at least 30-60 seconds
rationale: Weight shifts every 15-30 minutes: forward lean (60 seconds), side lean (30 seconds each), or push-up (30-60 seconds). Consistent schedule prevents pressure injuries over bony prominences.

stem: A client with a median nerve injury at the wrist will demonstrate weakness in which functional activity?
options: Thumb opposition and precision grip (difficulty with fine pinch activities)
rationale: Median nerve at wrist: thenar muscle paralysis (opponens pollicis, abductor pollicis brevis) causing loss of thumb opposition. Affects tip pinch, fine manipulation, and precision grip.

stem: Which documentation abbreviation means 'as needed'?
rationale: PRN = pro re nata (as needed). Other common abbreviations: q.d. (daily), b.i.d. (twice daily), t.i.d. (three times daily), stat (immediately).

stem: An OTA is conducting a group session for clients with chronic mental illness. Which group protocol component is MOST important?
options: Consistent structure with warm-up, purposeful activity, processing/discussion, and closure/summary
rationale: Group therapy structure: opening (warm-up, orientation), activity (purposeful, graded), processing (discussion of experience), and closure (summary, review, link to goals). Consistency provides safety.

stem: A client with a high-level tetraplegia (C4) needs an environmental control unit (ECU). Which access method is appropriate?
options: Sip-and-puff, head movement, or voice-activated control system
rationale: C4 tetraplegia has no hand/arm function. ECU access: sip-and-puff (breath control), head switches, voice activation, eye gaze, or chin-controlled systems for controlling environment.

stem: Which developmental milestone indicates readiness for toilet training?
options: Ability to sit independently, awareness of wetness/fullness, ability to follow simple directions (18-24 months)
rationale: Toilet training readiness (typically 18-24 months): independent sitting, awareness of bladder/bowel fullness, ability to follow directions, staying dry for 2+ hours, and showing interest.

stem: An OTA is fabricating a wrist cock-up splint. The wrist should be positioned in approximately:
rationale: A wrist cock-up splint positions the wrist in 20-30° extension (functional position), which optimizes tendon mechanics for grip and reduces carpal tunnel pressure.

stem: A client with generalized anxiety disorder reports inability to focus during therapy. Which grounding technique should the OTA teach?
options: 5-4-3-2-1 grounding technique: identify 5 things you see, 4 hear, 3 touch, 2 smell, 1 taste
rationale: The 5-4-3-2-1 grounding technique uses sensory awareness to redirect attention from anxiety to the present moment. It provides an immediate, portable coping strategy.

stem: Which billing code represents a 15-minute unit of therapeutic activity in OT?
options: 97530 (therapeutic activities — functional tasks directed at a specific functional outcome)
rationale: CPT 97530: therapeutic activities involving dynamic activities to improve functional performance (e.g., simulated ADL tasks, transfer training). Billed in 15-minute units.

stem: An OTA is training a client with hemiplegia in kitchen safety. Which adaptation is MOST important?
options: Use dycem/non-slip mat to stabilize items, one-handed cutting board with spikes, and organize items at accessible height
Cook only with a caregiver present
rationale: Hemiplegic kitchen safety: non-slip mat (dycem), adapted cutting boards (rocker knife, spikes), electric appliances, organize at waist height, and use timers for safety.

stem: A client with myasthenia gravis has fluctuating weakness that worsens with activity. How should the OTA schedule therapy?
options: Morning sessions when the client is strongest, with frequent rest breaks and energy conservation throughout
rationale: Myasthenia gravis: schedule therapy when strongest (usually morning, after medication peak), use energy conservation, incorporate rest breaks, and prioritize most important activities first.

stem: Which wheelchair feature allows a client to elevate the footrests for lower extremity edema management?
options: Elevating leg rests that support the lower legs in an elevated position
rationale: Elevating leg rests (ELRs) allow the legs to be raised above heart level for edema management, post-surgical elevation requirements, and clients who cannot tolerate sitting with feet dependent.

stem: An OTA is documenting a client's progress note. Which statement demonstrates skilled intervention?
options: OTA provided graded therapeutic activities for R UE, progressing from assisted to independent donning of sock with modified technique, requiring 2 verbal cues for sequencing
rationale: Skilled documentation specifies: what was done (graded therapeutic activities), how (progressing difficulty), outcome (independent with modification), and cues needed (2 verbal).

stem: A client with a wrist fracture is 8 weeks post-ORIF and has significant stiffness. Which modality combination is MOST effective before stretching?
options: Moist heat or paraffin wax followed by AROM, gentle PROM, and joint mobilization
rationale: Heat modalities (moist heat, paraffin) increase tissue extensibility before stretching. Follow with AROM, gentle PROM, and joint mobilization within tolerance to restore motion.

stem: Which reflex, if persistent beyond 4-6 months, may interfere with midline hand use and bilateral activities in infants?
options: Asymmetric tonic neck reflex (ATNR) — turns head causes arm extension on face side
rationale: Persistent ATNR causes arm extension when the head turns to that side, preventing midline hand use, bilateral manipulation, and visual tracking of objects across midline.

stem: A client with tremor from essential tremor has difficulty with self-feeding. Which adaptation is MOST helpful?
options: Weighted utensils, non-slip mat under the plate, plate guard, and built-up handles
rationale: Weighted utensils dampen tremor amplitude through proprioceptive input. Additional aids: non-slip mat (plate stability), plate guard (prevents spillage), and built-up handles (easier grip).

stem: An OTA is teaching a client post-TBI to use a memory notebook. What sections should be included?
options: Calendar, daily schedule, to-do lists, personal information, therapy notes, and log of completed activities
rationale: Memory notebook sections: orientation (personal info), calendar/schedule, to-do lists, daily log (completed activities), therapy notes, medication schedule, and important contacts.

stem: Which standardized assessment measures upper extremity motor function after stroke?
options: Fugl-Meyer Assessment (upper extremity) — evaluates motor function, sensation, balance, and ROM
rationale: The Fugl-Meyer UE assesses motor function through a hierarchy of tasks from reflexes to voluntary movement, with a maximum score of 66 for the upper extremity section.

stem: A client requires a built-up handle for eating utensils. Which condition is this MOST commonly used for?
options: Decreased grip strength or limited finger flexion (arthritis, weakness, nerve injury)
rationale: Built-up handles increase the diameter of utensils, reducing the grip force required. Used for arthritis, grip weakness, nerve injuries, and decreased finger ROM.

stem: An OTA is treating a burn client in the acute phase. What is the anti-deformity position for the neck?
options: Neutral to slight extension with no pillow (prevents flexion contracture of anterior neck)
rationale: Neck burns: position in neutral to slight extension (no pillow) to prevent flexion contracture. Anterior neck burns scar into flexion, limiting head extension and neck ROM.

stem: Which occupation-based assessment evaluates a client's satisfaction with their occupational performance?
options: Canadian Occupational Performance Measure (COPM) — client identifies and rates importance, performance, and satisfaction
rationale: The COPM is a client-centered, semi-structured interview identifying occupational performance issues. Clients rate importance (1-10) and their performance and satisfaction (1-10) for each issue.

stem: A client with a radial nerve injury (high) demonstrates which functional deficit?
options: Wrist drop (inability to extend the wrist), finger drop, and loss of thumb extension
rationale: High radial nerve injury: wrist drop (loss of wrist extensors), finger drop (loss of finger extensors), and loss of thumb extension. The client cannot open the hand or stabilize the wrist.

stem: An OTA is running a life skills group for adolescents with intellectual disabilities. Which activity addresses community participation?
options: Simulated grocery shopping, public transportation navigation, and restaurant ordering with role-play
rationale: Community participation skills: simulated real-world tasks (grocery shopping, transportation, ordering food) through role-play, community outings, and gradually increasing independence.

stem: A client with lymphedema of the upper extremity should follow which precaution during daily activities?
options: Avoid blood pressure cuffs, needle sticks, and constrictive clothing on the affected arm; elevate when resting
Apply tight compression at all times
Exercise vigorously with the affected arm
rationale: Lymphedema precautions: avoid BP cuffs, needle sticks, cuts/burns on the affected arm, constrictive clothing/jewelry, excessive heat, and heavy lifting. Elevate when resting.

stem: Which type of grasp is used when holding a key to turn a lock?
options: Lateral (key) pinch — thumb pad presses against the lateral side of the index finger
rationale: Lateral (key) pinch: the thumb pad presses against the radial side of the index finger. Used for keys, cards, and thin flat objects. Tested with a pinch gauge.

stem: A client with ALS (amyotrophic lateral sclerosis) is losing hand function. Which AT device maintains communication independence?
options: Eye-gaze communication system or speech-generating device with switch access
rationale: As ALS progresses, eye-gaze technology and switch-accessed speech-generating devices maintain communication when hand function is lost. Plan proactively for progressive decline.

stem: An OTA documents 'Client demonstrates modified independence with bathing — requires shower chair and long-handled sponge.' What FIM score is this?
options: 6 (modified independence — requires device but no helper)
rationale: FIM 6 (modified independence): the client performs the activity independently but requires adaptive equipment, takes extra time, or has safety concerns. No helper is needed.

stem: Which approach is MOST appropriate for a client with constructional apraxia who has difficulty with dressing?
options: Lay out clothing in the order of donning, use consistent routine, label inside/outside of garments, and provide visual templates
rationale: Constructional apraxia dressing strategies: lay out clothing in sequence, use color-coded labels (inside/outside, front/back), consistent routine, and visual/tactile cues.

stem: A client presents with boutonniere deformity of the finger. What is the characteristic posture?
options: PIP flexion with DIP hyperextension due to disruption of the central slip
rationale: Boutonniere deformity: central slip disruption at PIP → PIP flexion + DIP hyperextension (lateral bands migrate volar). Treatment: PIP extension splint, DIP flexion exercises.

stem: An OTA is teaching a client with visual field loss to scan for safety during community mobility. Which technique is essential?
options: Systematic head-turning/scanning toward the blind field before crossing streets, opening doors, and navigating crowds
rationale: Visual field loss scanning: systematic head-turning toward the blind field, pause before transitions (crossings, doors), scan before moving, and practice in progressively complex environments.

stem: A client recovering from a flexor tendon repair at 6 weeks can begin which type of exercise?
options: Place-and-hold exercises (passive flexion with active hold) and gentle active flexion within protected range
rationale: At 6 weeks post-flexor tendon repair: begin place-and-hold (passive flexion, then hold actively) and gentle active flexion. Resistance typically begins at 8-10 weeks.

stem: Which neurodevelopmental treatment (NDT/Bobath) technique is used to inhibit abnormal tone before facilitating normal movement?
options: Weight-bearing through the affected extremity, slow sustained stretching, and reflex-inhibiting positions
rationale: NDT tone inhibition: weight-bearing (inhibits flexor tone in UE), slow sustained stretch, reflex-inhibiting positions, and trunk rotation. These prepare for facilitation of normal movement.

stem: A client with depression has decreased motivation for ADL participation. Which motivational strategy is MOST effective?
options: Offer choices, set small achievable goals, connect activities to personal values, and provide positive reinforcement
rationale: Depression motivation: offer limited choices (autonomy), break tasks into small achievable steps, connect to personal values/goals, positive reinforcement, and collaborate on meaningful activities.

stem: Which home modification is MOST important for a client returning home after a hip fracture?
options: Remove throw rugs, install grab bars in bathroom, ensure adequate lighting, and place frequently used items at accessible height
rationale: Post-hip fracture home modifications: remove trip hazards, install bathroom grab bars, improve lighting (nightlights), accessible item placement, raised toilet seat, and shower chair.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has difficulty with visual-motor integration. Which activity addresses this deficit?
options: Tracing, copying shapes, bead stringing, maze completion, and coloring within lines
rationale: Visual-motor integration activities: tracing (guided), copying (increasing complexity), bead stringing, mazes, coloring within boundaries, cutting, and puzzles — all require visual input guiding motor output.

stem: A client with a spinal cord injury at C8-T1 has intact finger flexors but weak intrinsic muscles. Which grasp pattern is most affected?
options: Precision pinch (tip-to-tip and three-jaw chuck) due to intrinsic muscle weakness
rationale: C8-T1 SCI: finger flexors intact but intrinsic muscles (lumbricals, interossei) are weak. This affects precision pinch, lateral pinch, and fine manipulation while gross grasp is relatively preserved.

stem: Which ergonomic workstation modification reduces the risk of carpal tunnel syndrome?
options: Neutral wrist position with keyboard at elbow height, wrist rest, frequent breaks, and proper chair height
rationale: CTS prevention: neutral wrist (slight extension), keyboard at elbow height, padded wrist rest, regular breaks with stretching, ergonomic mouse, and proper seating posture.

stem: A client with osteoarthritis of the CMC joint of the thumb has difficulty with jar opening. Which adaptive equipment helps?
options: Rubber jar opener, electric jar opener, or mounted jar opener to reduce joint stress and torque
rationale: CMC OA adaptations: rubber jar openers (increase friction, reduce force), electric/mounted jar openers, enlarged handles, and joint protection techniques to reduce thumb joint stress.

stem: An OTA working in a skilled nursing facility must comply with which Medicare reimbursement requirement?
options: Services must be skilled (requiring OT expertise), medically necessary, and documented with functional progress
rationale: Medicare SNF reimbursement requires: skilled service (requires OT expertise), medical necessity, functional documentation, reasonable and predictable progress, and compliance with coverage criteria.

stem: A client with Dupuytren's contracture has difficulty extending the ring and little fingers. Which intervention is appropriate?
options: Extension splinting, gentle AROM, scar management post-surgery, and functional activities emphasizing finger extension
rationale: Dupuytren's contracture treatment: pre-surgical — extension splinting, stretching; post-surgical (fasciectomy) — extension splinting, scar management, AROM, edema control, and functional activities.

stem: Which documentation format organizes information as Subjective, Objective, Assessment, and Plan?
rationale: SOAP: Subjective (client's report), Objective (measurable findings/interventions), Assessment (clinical reasoning/progress), Plan (next session's plan/goals). Standard PT/OT documentation.

stem: A client with schizophrenia in a community mental health setting needs help with IADL skills. Which OT model guides intervention?
options: Model of Human Occupation (MOHO) — addresses volition, habituation, and performance capacity in context
rationale: MOHO considers volition (motivation/interests), habituation (roles/routines), performance capacity (skills), and environment. It guides holistic intervention for mental health community integration.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with complex regional pain syndrome. Which sign distinguishes CRPS from other pain conditions?
options: Disproportionate pain, edema, temperature/color changes, and trophic changes in an entire region (not following nerve distribution)
Pain only at the injury site
rationale: CRPS features: pain disproportionate to injury, regional distribution (not following a nerve), edema, temperature asymmetry, color changes, sudomotor changes, and trophic changes.

stem: A child demonstrates difficulty crossing midline during bilateral activities. Which underlying deficit might this indicate?
options: Incomplete lateral dominance or poor bilateral integration and sequencing
rationale: Midline crossing difficulty may indicate: incomplete lateral dominance, poor bilateral integration, retained ATNR, or sensory processing issues. It affects handwriting, dressing, and bilateral play.

stem: Which splint is designed to substitute for weak or absent wrist extensors and maintain functional wrist position?
options: Wrist cock-up splint (volar or dorsal) maintaining 20-30° extension
rationale: A wrist cock-up splint holds the wrist in 20-30° extension, substituting for weak extensors and providing a stable base for hand function. Used for radial nerve palsy, wrist weakness.

stem: An OTA is teaching stress management techniques to a client with chronic pain. Which technique has the BEST evidence?
options: Progressive muscle relaxation, diaphragmatic breathing, mindfulness meditation, and cognitive restructuring
rationale: Evidence-based stress management for chronic pain: PMR (systematically tensing/releasing muscles), diaphragmatic breathing, mindfulness, guided imagery, and cognitive behavioral strategies.

stem: A client with a swan neck deformity of the finger shows which joint posture?
options: PIP hyperextension with DIP flexion due to volar plate laxity and lateral band migration
rationale: Swan neck deformity: PIP hyperextension + DIP flexion. Caused by volar plate laxity, intrinsic tightness, or FDS rupture. Treatment: figure-of-eight ring splint or silver ring splint.

stem: When working with a client who has cultural beliefs that differ from Western medical practice, the OTA should:
options: Respect cultural beliefs, integrate them into treatment when safe, ask about preferences, and collaborate with cultural sensitivity
rationale: Cultural competence: respect beliefs, ask about health practices and preferences, integrate cultural values into treatment when safe, use interpreters when needed, and avoid assumptions.

stem: A client needs to learn one-handed shoe tying after a CVA. Which technique does the OTA teach?
options: One-handed shoe-tying technique using loops and hooks, or recommend elastic shoelaces/slip-on shoes as alternative
Have someone else tie shoes always
rationale: One-handed shoe tying: specialized technique using loops. Alternatives: elastic no-tie laces, Velcro-strap shoes, slip-on shoes, or long-handled shoe horn with elastic laces.

stem: Which evidence-based intervention for constraint-induced movement therapy (CIMT) in chronic stroke requires the client to have at least:
options: 10° active wrist extension and 10° active finger extension in the affected hand
rationale: CIMT inclusion criteria: 10°+ active wrist extension, 10°+ active finger extension (at least 2 fingers). This minimal active movement threshold ensures the client can participate in intensive training.

stem: An OTA is performing a home safety evaluation. Which area has the HIGHEST fall risk?
options: Bathroom (wet surfaces, transfers, confined space, lack of grab bars)
rationale: The bathroom is the highest fall-risk area: wet/slippery surfaces, confined space, transfers (tub/toilet), and often lacks grab bars. Falls here often result in serious injury.

stem: A client with a total shoulder replacement (TSA) has which post-surgical precaution?
options: Avoid combined shoulder extension, internal rotation, and adduction (posterior approach); limit active ROM per surgeon protocol
rationale: TSA precautions: avoid posterior dislocation forces (extension + IR + adduction), limit active elevation per protocol, use sling as prescribed, and avoid pushing/pulling with the affected arm.

stem: Which wheelchair measurement ensures proper seat depth to prevent pressure behind the knees?
options: Posterior buttock to popliteal fossa minus 1-2 inches to prevent popliteal pressure
rationale: Seat depth: measure posterior buttock to popliteal fossa minus 1-2 inches. Too deep causes popliteal pressure (DVT risk); too short reduces weight distribution and stability.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who demonstrates perseveration after TBI. Which strategy helps redirect the client?
options: Provide a clear verbal cue to stop, redirect to a different activity, and use environmental changes to break the pattern
rationale: Perseveration management: firm, calm verbal cue to stop, change the activity or environment, provide structured transitions, and use external cues to redirect attention.

stem: A client needs to perform self-catheterization but has limited hand function due to C6 SCI. Which adaptation enables independence?
options: Catheter with loop handle or adapted grip, mirror for visualization, and Velcro leg strap for catheter management
rationale: Adapted self-catheterization for C6 SCI: loop handle on catheter (tenodesis grasp), mirror for visualization, adaptive leg bag management, and proper positioning (supine or sitting).

stem: Which pediatric assessment evaluates sensory processing patterns through caregiver questionnaire?
options: Sensory Profile (Dunn) — caregiver questionnaire identifying sensory processing patterns and their functional impact
rationale: Dunn's Sensory Profile: caregiver questionnaire evaluating sensory processing patterns across sensory systems and behavioral responses. Identifies sensory seeking, avoiding, sensitivity, and registration patterns.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder). During the 'frozen' phase, what is the focus?
options: Pain management, gentle ROM within tolerance, and maintaining function through compensatory strategies
rationale: Frozen phase: joint is stiffest, pain moderate. Focus on pain management, gentle AROM/PROM within tolerance, activity modification, and joint protection until the thawing phase begins.

stem: Which type of transfer is used when a client cannot stand and requires maximum assistance?
options: Dependent lift transfer or mechanical lift (Hoyer lift)
rationale: When a client cannot bear weight and requires maximum assistance: mechanical lift (Hoyer) is safest for both client and caregiver. This prevents injury from manual dependent lifting.

stem: A client demonstrates difficulty with topographical orientation after a right CVA. What does this mean?
options: Difficulty navigating familiar environments and finding their way around the hospital/home
rationale: Topographical disorientation (right hemisphere CVA): inability to navigate previously familiar environments. Interventions: verbal landmarks, written directions, maps, consistent routes.

stem: Which evidence-based approach for upper extremity rehabilitation after stroke involves robotic devices to assist movement?
options: Robot-assisted therapy providing repetitive, intensive task-specific upper extremity training with augmented feedback
rationale: Robot-assisted therapy provides high-repetition, task-specific UE training with augmented feedback. Evidence shows improvement in motor function when combined with conventional therapy.

stem: An OTA is fabricating a splint and notices the thermoplastic material is too thick for the client's small hand. What should the done?
options: Select a thinner gauge thermoplastic appropriate for the hand size and application (1/16" for fingers, 1/8" for forearm-based splints)
rationale: Thermoplastic thickness: 1/16" (thin) for finger splints and small hands, 1/8" (standard) for forearm-based splints, 3/32" for medium applications. Match to body part and client size.

stem: A client in acute care is demonstrating agitation and confusion 3 days post-surgery. The OTA suspects delirium. What is the appropriate response?
options: Reduce stimulation, reorient the client, maintain safety, and report findings to the OT and nursing team immediately
rationale: Post-operative delirium: reduce stimulation, provide orientation cues, ensure safety, and report to nursing/physician immediately. Delirium is a medical emergency requiring workup.

stem: Which motor learning concept suggests that practicing a task in its actual context produces better learning than practicing components separately?
options: Whole-task practice with contextual relevance (ecological approach to motor learning)
rationale: The ecological approach: practicing whole tasks in natural contexts produces better transfer than practicing components in isolation. ADL training in the actual environment is more effective.

stem: A client with carpal tunnel syndrome has a positive Phalen's test. What did the test involve?
options: Sustained wrist flexion for 60 seconds producing numbness/tingling in the median nerve distribution
rationale: Phalen's test: sustained wrist flexion for 60 seconds. Positive if numbness/tingling in the median nerve distribution (thumb, index, middle, radial ring finger) is reproduced.

stem: An OTA is discharging a client who needs continued home services. What must be included in the discharge documentation?
options: Current functional status, goals achieved/not achieved, home program with instructions, equipment recommendations, and follow-up plan
rationale: Discharge documentation: current functional status, goals met/unmet, HEP with written/visual instructions, equipment recommendations, precautions, and follow-up referral information.

stem: Which intervention approach is BEST for a client with learned nonuse of the hemiplegic upper extremity?
options: Modified constraint-induced movement therapy (mCIMT) or intensive task-specific training of the affected arm
Focus only on the unaffected arm
rationale: Learned nonuse: the client avoids using the affected arm due to initial difficulty. mCIMT addresses this by restraining the unaffected arm and intensively training the affected arm in functional tasks.

stem: A client has been referred for driver rehabilitation assessment after a CVA. Which cognitive skills are essential for safe driving?
options: Attention (sustained, divided, selective), visual scanning, processing speed, judgment, problem-solving, and reaction time
rationale: Safe driving requires: sustained and divided attention, rapid visual scanning, processing speed, judgment/decision-making, problem-solving, spatial awareness, and adequate reaction time.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with a healing skin graft over the dorsal hand. Which intervention prevents adhesion?
options: Gentle AROM/PROM of uninvolved joints, edema control, scar management (silicone, massage after graft stabilized), and light functional use
rationale: Post-skin graft: protect graft initially, then gentle motion as healed, scar management (silicone sheets, gentle massage after 2-3 weeks), edema control, and gradual functional use.

stem: Which safety consideration is MOST important when using a hot pack (hydrocollator pack) with an elderly client?
options: Ensure adequate layers between the skin and hot pack (6-8 layers of toweling), check skin every 5 minutes, and avoid areas with decreased sensation
rationale: Hot pack safety: 6-8 towel layers, check skin every 5 minutes (especially elderly with thin skin/decreased sensation), avoid insensate areas, and never leave the client unattended.

stem: A client with a spinal cord injury is at risk for autonomic dysreflexia. Which SCI level is at risk and what are the signs?
options: T6 and above — sudden severe hypertension, pounding headache, flushing above the level, sweating, bradycardia
rationale: Autonomic dysreflexia: T6 and above. Triggered by noxious stimuli below the injury (full bladder, constipation, pressure ulcer). Emergency: sit up, remove cause, monitor BP, seek medical help if persistent.

stem: An OTA is teaching joint protection techniques to a client with RA. Which principle applies to carrying objects?
options: Use two hands or forearms to carry, distribute weight evenly, and use a cart when possible to avoid stress on finger joints
Carry as much as possible at once
rationale: RA carrying techniques: use two hands/forearms, distribute weight evenly, use a cart/bag with shoulder strap, avoid carrying heavy loads in hands, and make multiple trips.

stem: Which clinical reasoning process involves the OTA considering the client's life narrative and personal meaning of occupations?
options: Narrative reasoning — understanding the client's life story, values, and the meaning they assign to occupations
rationale: Narrative reasoning: understanding the client's life story, personal meaning of activities, and how illness/disability has disrupted their narrative. It guides client-centered, meaningful intervention.

stem: A client needs to use a computer but has limited fine motor control. Which input device modification is MOST helpful?
options: Enlarged keyboard, trackball mouse, sticky keys, word prediction software, and/or voice recognition
rationale: Computer access for limited fine motor: enlarged keyboard, trackball/joystick mouse, sticky keys (eliminates simultaneous key presses), word prediction, and voice recognition software.

stem: An OTA notices a client's incision site is red, warm, and has purulent drainage. What is the appropriate action?
options: Stop treatment, document findings, and immediately notify the OT and nursing for wound evaluation — signs suggest infection
Ignore it if the client feels fine
rationale: Signs of wound infection: redness, warmth, purulent drainage, and possible fever. Stop treatment, document findings, and notify the medical team immediately for evaluation and treatment.

stem: A client with bilateral hand arthritis needs to open containers. Which joint protection strategy reduces MCP stress?
options: Use palm and forearm to twist, use electric openers, and leverage body weight rather than hand grip alone
rationale: Container opening with arthritis: use palms (not fingers), lever tools, electric openers, non-slip mats for grip, and body weight leverage. Minimize repetitive twisting forces on MCP joints.

stem: Which outcome measure assesses occupational performance and satisfaction in a client-centered manner?
rationale: The COPM identifies client-selected occupational performance problems and rates importance, performance, and satisfaction on 1-10 scales. It tracks change over time from the client's perspective.

stem: A child with Down syndrome is working on feeding independence. Which utensil adaptation is MOST helpful?
options: Weighted spoon with built-up handle and angled design to compensate for reduced grip and coordination
rationale: Down syndrome feeding: weighted spoon (proprioceptive feedback), built-up handle (easier grip for hypotonia), angled/bent spoon (compensates for limited wrist rotation), and non-slip mat.

stem: An OTA is preparing a client for community reintegration after TBI. Which IADL should be assessed in the actual community setting?
options: Grocery shopping, money handling, public transportation use, and community navigation
rationale: Community IADL assessment: grocery shopping (planning, money, navigation), transportation (route planning, safety), money management, and community navigation (orientation, safety awareness).

stem: Which proprioceptive neuromuscular facilitation (PNF) technique uses alternating isometric contractions to improve stability?
options: Rhythmic stabilization — alternating isometric contractions in opposing directions without movement
rationale: Rhythmic stabilization: alternating isometric contractions in opposing directions (flexion/extension, rotation) without allowing movement. Promotes co-contraction and stability.

stem: A client with severe spasticity in the elbow flexors is having difficulty with hygiene in the elbow crease. What is the OTA's intervention?
options: Use an elbow extension splint for positioning, inhibitory techniques (slow sustained stretch, weight-bearing), and educate caregivers on hygiene access
rationale: Spastic elbow flexor management: positioning splint (serial casting or extension splint), inhibitory techniques, skin care in the elbow crease, and caregiver education on gentle range and hygiene.

stem: An OTA is working in a school setting with a child who has difficulty with scissors use. Which underlying skill deficit might cause this?
options: Bilateral coordination (stabilize with one hand, cut with the other), in-hand manipulation, and visual-motor integration
rationale: Scissor use requires: bilateral coordination, visual-motor integration, hand separation (stabilizing with ulnar fingers, manipulating with radial fingers), and graded force control.

stem: Which documentation practice ensures compliance with HIPAA privacy regulations?
options: Document only relevant clinical information, store records securely, share only with authorized treatment team, and obtain consent for release
Post client info on social media
rationale: HIPAA compliance: minimum necessary information, secure storage, authorized access only, consent for release, no discussion in public areas, and proper disposal of PHI.

stem: A geriatric client with osteoporosis should be positioned how during OT activities?
options: Maintain upright posture, avoid trunk flexion and twisting under load, and use proper body mechanics during all activities
rationale: Osteoporosis positioning: maintain upright posture (avoid kyphosis progression), avoid trunk flexion under load (fracture risk), use extension-based posture training, and proper body mechanics.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with Guillain-Barré syndrome in the recovery phase. Which exercise progression is appropriate?
options: Begin with AROM within tolerance, progress to light resistance, monitor for overwork damage, and include energy conservation
rationale: GBS recovery: gentle AROM → light PRE (avoiding overwork damage), energy conservation, graded functional activities, and monitoring for fatigue. Overwork can damage regenerating nerves.

stem: Which assistive technology device allows a client with high-level tetraplegia to control their smartphone?
options: Head-controlled mouse, sip-and-puff switch, or voice control (Siri/Google Assistant)
rationale: Smartphone access for high tetraplegia: voice control (Siri/Google), head-controlled mouse systems, sip-and-puff switches, eye-gaze technology, and mouth stick stylus.

stem: A client recovering from a TBI demonstrates impulsivity during cooking tasks. Which safety strategy should the OTA implement?
options: Structured step-by-step checklists, verbal stop cues before each step, close supervision, and environmental safety setup
rationale: TBI impulsivity in cooking: structured checklists (external executive function support), verbal stop-check cues, close supervision, safety setup (remove sharp objects initially), and graded independence.

stem: Which muscle testing grade indicates that a client can move through full ROM against gravity but not against resistance?
rationale: MMT grades: 0 = no contraction, 1 = trace (palpable), 2 = full ROM gravity-eliminated, 3 = full ROM against gravity, 4 = full ROM against moderate resistance, 5 = full ROM against maximum resistance.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with a healed burn on the hand. When should scar massage begin?
options: After the wound is fully closed and healed (typically 2-3 weeks post-closure), with gentle pressure progressing to deeper massage
rationale: Scar massage begins once the wound is fully closed (no open areas): start with gentle pressure, progress to deeper massage, use moisturizer, and perform 5-10 minutes several times daily.

stem: A client with chronic fatigue syndrome is learning to manage daily activities. Which concept is central to their OT plan?
options: Activity pacing, energy conservation, graded activity progression, and activity-rest cycling to avoid post-exertional malaise
rationale: CFS management: activity pacing (stay within energy envelope), graded activity, energy conservation, activity-rest cycling, and avoiding post-exertional malaise (PEM) triggers.

stem: Which hand therapy technique uses continuous passive motion to prevent joint stiffness after surgery?
options: Continuous passive motion (CPM) machine that moves the joint through a controlled ROM without active effort
rationale: CPM machines provide continuous passive joint motion to prevent adhesions, maintain ROM, and promote healing. Commonly used after TKR, finger joint surgery, and elbow surgery.

stem: An OTA is working with a child who has sensory defensiveness to touch. What is the first step in desensitization?
options: Begin with deep pressure/proprioceptive input (preferred by the child), then gradually introduce lighter touch textures at the child's pace
Start with the most aversive textures
rationale: Tactile defensiveness desensitization: start with deep pressure (calming input), then introduce tolerable textures, gradually progress to lighter/more varied textures, always at the child's pace and comfort.

stem: A client with MCP joint replacements (arthroplasty) should avoid which movement?
options: Lateral stress (ulnar/radial deviation), forceful gripping, and resistive pinch that stress the prosthetic joints
rationale: Post-MCP arthroplasty: avoid lateral stress (deviation), forceful gripping, and resistive pinch. Use dynamic extension splint as prescribed, gentle AROM, and gradually progress function.

stem: Which clinical finding indicates that an arterial line is present and the OTA should take precautions during treatment?
options: A catheter inserted in the radial artery (wrist) connected to a pressure transducer for continuous BP monitoring
A pulse oximeter on the finger
rationale: An arterial line (a-line) in the radial artery provides continuous BP monitoring. Precautions: avoid bending the wrist, be careful with positioning, and do not apply pressure to the a-line site.

stem: An OTA is providing caregiver training for a client with advanced dementia. Which topic is MOST critical?
options: Safe transfer techniques, positioning for skin integrity, feeding assistance, and recognizing signs of distress
rationale: Advanced dementia caregiver training: safe transfers (gait belt use), skin integrity (repositioning schedule), feeding assistance (dysphagia precautions), and recognizing pain/distress behaviors.

stem: Which outcome measure is specifically designed for hand therapy and measures function after hand/wrist injuries?
options: Disabilities of the Arm, Shoulder and Hand (DASH) questionnaire
rationale: The DASH is a 30-item self-report questionnaire measuring physical function and symptoms in upper extremity musculoskeletal disorders. It is validated for hand, wrist, and arm conditions.

stem: A client in a psychiatric setting refuses to participate in a group. What is the OTA's BEST response?
options: Respect the client's autonomy, explore the reason for refusal, offer alternative individual activities, and document the refusal
rationale: Respect autonomy: explore reasons (anxiety, past experience, symptoms), offer alternatives, document refusal and discussion, inform the OT, and try again later without coercion.

stem: Which visual perception deficit causes difficulty distinguishing an object from its background?
options: Figure-ground discrimination deficit — difficulty separating foreground from background
rationale: Figure-ground: difficulty picking out items from a cluttered background (finding white socks in a white drawer, utensils on a cluttered counter). Use high-contrast, organized environments.

stem: An OTA is educating a client about sleep hygiene for improved occupational performance. Which recommendation is evidence-based?
options: Consistent sleep/wake times, dark cool room, avoid screens 1 hour before bed, limit caffeine, and relaxation routine
rationale: Sleep hygiene: consistent schedule, cool/dark/quiet room, avoid screens 1hr before bed, limit caffeine after noon, relaxation routine (reading, meditation), and avoid large meals before bed.

stem: A client with a nerve transfer surgery for brachial plexus injury needs which early intervention?
options: Edema management, gentle PROM, sensory re-education, and motor re-education guided by nerve regeneration timeline
rationale: Post-nerve transfer: protect the surgical site, edema management, gentle PROM (prevent stiffness), sensory re-education as nerve regenerates, and motor re-education (guided by EMG/nerve conduction).

stem: Which hand intrinsic muscle test asks the client to spread the fingers apart against resistance?
options: Dorsal interossei test (finger abduction against resistance)
rationale: Dorsal interossei (ulnar nerve): finger abduction (spreading fingers apart) against resistance. Weakness indicates ulnar nerve dysfunction. Test each finger separately.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with chronic pain using a cognitive-behavioral approach. Which strategy is included?
options: Activity pacing, thought restructuring (identifying and challenging pain catastrophizing), relaxation training, and goal-setting
rationale: CBT for chronic pain: activity pacing, cognitive restructuring (challenging catastrophizing thoughts), relaxation training, graded activity exposure, and collaborative goal-setting.

stem: A client needs wheelchair propulsion training. Which muscle group is primarily used during the push phase?
options: Shoulder flexors, elbow extensors (triceps), and anterior deltoid for the push phase
rationale: Wheelchair push phase: shoulder flexors/anterior deltoid initiate the push, triceps extend the elbow for propulsion force. Recovery phase uses shoulder extensors and biceps.

stem: Which standardized assessment evaluates a child's ability to perform self-care, mobility, and social function?
options: Pediatric Evaluation of Disability Inventory (PEDI) — measures functional capabilities and caregiver assistance in children
rationale: The PEDI evaluates children aged 6 months-7.5 years across self-care, mobility, and social function domains. It measures both capability and caregiver assistance levels.

stem: A client with bilateral upper extremity amputations needs to perform toileting independently. Which adaptive solution is available?
options: Adapted toileting systems with bidets, environmental controls, and prosthetic options; OT addresses each client individually
rationale: Bilateral UE amputee toileting: bidet for cleansing, adaptive toileting devices, environmental modifications, prosthetic use training, and individualized problem-solving for independence.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has anosognosia after a right CVA. What is this condition?
options: Lack of awareness or denial of one's own neurological deficit (e.g., not recognizing hemiplegia)
rationale: Anosognosia: lack of awareness of one's own deficit (common after right CVA). The client may deny hemiplegia or neglect. This impacts safety and participation in rehabilitation.

stem: Which infection prevention measure applies when transporting a client on contact precautions through the hallway?
options: Cover the client's wound/affected area, perform hand hygiene, and minimize contact with hallway surfaces
rationale: Transporting contact precaution clients: cover the affected area, clean hands before leaving the room, minimize hallway contact, clean transport equipment after use.

stem: A client with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease should learn which breathing technique for use during ADL activities?
options: Pursed-lip breathing: inhale through the nose (2 counts), exhale through pursed lips (4 counts); exhale during exertion
rationale: Pursed-lip breathing maintains positive airway pressure, prevents airway collapse, improves gas exchange. Coordinate: inhale before effort, exhale (pursed lips) during exertion.

stem: An OTA is working with an older adult on fall prevention. Which balance exercise has the strongest evidence for reducing falls?
options: Tai Chi — combines balance, weight shifting, coordination, and strength in a progressive, supervised program
rationale: Tai Chi has strong evidence for fall prevention: improves balance, lower extremity strength, flexibility, and body awareness. Recommended 2-3x/week for at least 12 weeks.

stem: Which type of reasoning does the OTA use when considering insurance coverage, time constraints, and equipment availability?
options: Pragmatic reasoning — considering practical constraints and contextual factors
rationale: Pragmatic reasoning considers practical realities: insurance coverage, available equipment, time constraints, facility policies, staffing, and client resources that affect treatment options.

stem: A client with a healing fracture needs calcium and vitamin D for bone health. Which patient education is appropriate?
options: Educate about dietary sources (dairy, leafy greens, fortified foods) and suggest discussing supplementation with their physician
rationale: The OTA can educate about dietary sources of calcium and vitamin D within their scope. Supplement/medication recommendations must come from the physician or dietitian.
